title: Frozen Dataclasses

overview.what: A frozen dataclass is a dataclass where instances cannot be modified after initialization. It uses the `@dataclass(frozen=True)` decorator. It makes the class immutable, similar to a tuple, and automatically generates a `__hash__` method, allowing instances to be used as dictionary keys or stored in sets. Mental model: A frozen dataclass is a read-only container; once you lock it, any attempt to change its fields raises a FrozenInstanceError.
overview.why: Immutability prevents accidental state changes in data passed around your application. It also makes objects hashable, meaning they can be used in mappings or sets.

why_learning_this: You can safely pass data objects around without worrying about other functions mutating them.
You can use complex data structures as keys in dictionaries or elements in sets.

common_mistakes.title: Mutating internal lists
common_mistakes.explanation: A frozen dataclass prevents reassigning the field (e.g., `obj.items = []`), but if the field is a mutable object like a list, you can still mutate the list itself (`obj.items.append(1)`). Frozen only applies to the attributes directly on the class.

common_mistakes.title: Manual __hash__ conflicts
common_mistakes.explanation: Providing a custom `__hash__` or `__eq__` method can conflict with the one generated by `frozen=True`, leading to unexpected equality behaviors.

recall_questions.q: How do you make a dataclass immutable?
recall_questions.answer: By passing the `frozen=True` argument to the `@dataclass` decorator.

recall_questions.q: What additional magic method does `frozen=True` automatically generate?
recall_questions.answer: It automatically generates a `__hash__` method, making the instance hashable if its fields are also hashable.

recall_questions.q: Why would you want to make a dataclass frozen?
recall_questions.answer: To prevent accidental state mutation and to allow the instances to be used as dictionary keys or in sets.

understanding_checks.question: What is printed when this code is executed?
understanding_checks.why: Because `frozen=True` prevents reassignment of fields after the object is created. Attempting to assign `c.retries = 5` raises a `dataclasses.FrozenInstanceError`.

understanding_checks.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Team:
    name: str
    members: List[str]

t = Team('A', ['Alice'])
t.members.append('Bob')
print(len(t.members))
understanding_checks.question: What is printed when this code is executed?
understanding_checks.why: Frozen dataclasses only prevent reassignment of the attribute (`t.members = [...]`). They do not make the underlying mutable objects (like lists) immutable. The list itself can still be modified.

code_walkthrough.focus: points_set = {p1, p2} shows that frozen dataclasses are hashable and equal instances are deduplicated.

aha_moment.prediction: Will this successfully retrieve the value 42, or raise a KeyError because they are different objects?
aha_moment.common_guess: KeyError, because they are two different instances in memory.
aha_moment.why: It prints 42. `frozen=True` generates a `__hash__` method based on the fields. Since both instances have identical fields, they hash to the same value and `__eq__` evaluates to True, allowing them to act as identical dictionary keys.

practice_tasks.title: Secure the Configuration
practice_tasks.prompt: Given this standard dataclass `AppConfig`, modify it so that instances cannot be modified after initialization and can be used as dictionary keys.

challenge.title: Using Dataclasses in Sets
challenge.prompt: Create a frozen dataclass `UserIdentifier` with two string fields: `email` and `tenant_id`. Then, initialize a `set` containing two identical instances of `UserIdentifier` and print the length of the set.
